subject: Pulmonology
question: A 25-year-old previously healthy man presents with productive cough with green sputum, fever to 39°C, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain for 5 days. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. WBC 16,500, CRP 145. What is the most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?

media.description: CXR: Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms

options.explanation: For non-ICU CAP, ceftriaxone (covers typical bacteria: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) + azithromycin (covers atypicals: Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella) is standard. CURB-65 score 0 = outpatient management possible. Start antibiotics within 4 hours.

This is for healthcare-associated or hospital-acquired pneumonia (HCAP/HAP), or severe CAP with risk factors for MRSA/Pseudomonas. Unnecessary broad-spectrum coverage for community-acquired pneumonia in a healthy young patient.

Amoxicillin alone does NOT cover atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella) which are common in CAP in young adults. Combination therapy with a macrolide or doxycycline is recommended for atypical coverage.

Oseltamivir is antiviral for influenza, not antibacterial. CAP is bacterial (consolidation, leukocytosis, CRP). Antiviral only if influenza suspected during flu season, and bacterial coverage still needed.

This is the RIPE regimen for tuberculosis. TB typically has upper lobe involvement, cavitation, night sweats, weight loss, and slower onset over weeks-months, not acute presentation with lobar consolidation.

educationalObjective: Select appropriate empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia: cover typical bacteria AND atypical organisms. Use CURB-65 to determine treatment setting.
keyTakeaway: CAP treatment: Cover typicals (ceftriaxone) + atypicals (azithromycin). Start antibiotics within 4 hours. CURB-65 determines outpatient vs inpatient management.
